speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Foundation. The date is November 29, 1995. The survivor is Eva Wellner, nee Yoskowitz. The interviewer is Robert Clary. It's being interviewed in Laguna Hills, California, in the USA. And it's going to be in English. Today is November 29, 1995. My name is Robert Clary. I'm interviewing Eva Wellner in Laguna Hills, California, USA. And it's going to be in English. Can you tell me your first and last name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try, if I didn't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me where-- where you were born and what country, what city you were born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland. And what year were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what-- what month and date?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember when you were a child? Do you come from a big family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were five brothers and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the youngest? Where do you come?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the middle one? You were-- there are two brothers and then you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I had a sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: Chana. And then who else came?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And who else came after that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you sister was the youngest one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she was the oldest one. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the oldest, then came my brother, and I was the third.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came all brothers. Four brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what do you remember? Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they were born? Do you know where they were born, your grandparents? Were they born in Poland, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yes. It's Zawiercie. It's close to Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: On both sides of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't remember on both sides. We were-- I remember my father's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but very little. Not so close.

speaker: interviewer
text: They died before you grew up, is that it?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do? Do you know what business he was in?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a manufacturer of shoes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Slippers. And your mother was a housewife?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was [? Chaim ?] [? Yosef, ?] and my mother was Barbara.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do they call it in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Conservative, which means what? They went to synagogues on the holidays only?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos, yeah. But they didn't observe like those Chasidim.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did not go every-- every day to a synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak mostly Yiddish or a lot of Polish?

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a city was it where you were born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Big city? A lot of Jewish people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a place where there were a lot of Jews, or they were mixed?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your youth? Did you get along with your sisters and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a sister and her brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me. Tell me how. Tell me how you got along.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some days we fight, some days we're good. We were devoted to each--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a high school?

speaker: interviewer
text: No high school. When-- at what age did you finish going to school?

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't go to a high school. That's when you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When you were a child, who were your friends? Who were your friends there?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after you were out of school?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you play? What kind of-- what kind of play did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were playing and helping at home, my mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her day? Do you remember what she did all day long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she helped out my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And she was cooking and cleaning, and I helped with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And taking care of your seven children that she had?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the older the person, were they married young? Did they marry young, your sister and brother that were before you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was older than me, then I had a brother, and he was older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when she got married?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war she got married?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she stay in that city when she was married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Like, if I remember correct, but I-- no. Yes. They didn't go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you encounter anti-Semitism when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went to school, when I went to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were calling dirty Jew and throwing rocks at me. The school children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when they did that? What did you-- how did you react?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to defend myself, but there were more of them than me, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you defend yourself? Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- sometimes I threw back rocks. Or I didn't answer when they called me names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your friends were mostly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many there were, not where I went to school. I went in Oberschlesien.

speaker: interviewee
text: They moved away from Czestochowa, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it Upper Silesia that you moved?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? Do you remember when-- when you moved forward from that city?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you young when you moved? Were you still a young person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. In Czestochowa I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still-- you still-- you were still going to school when you moved?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to another school in another city?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the city that you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- first we lived in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we moved to Laurahutte. In German it was Laurahutte. And in Polish it was Siemianowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not going to ask you to spell that. Siemianowice. And that's in Upper Silesia?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think till we moved to Bedzin. I thought I knew everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You moved to-- you moved to Bedzin when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war broke out you moved to Bedzin. Why did you all move? Do you remember why you moved from one city to the other?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father felt because-- because there was too much anti-Semitism--

speaker: interviewee
text: He felt-- we were five brothers, so they need a Jewish education. And there, there wasn't too much Jewish education you could get--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to a smaller--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to a smaller city where you can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right. There was one, he was a rabbi, and he was the-- the-- the schochet, and he was everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: A schochet is somebody who would just kill the chickens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or see that the [INTERPOSING VOICES] meat is kosher. Is that what it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. Right. So he couldn't do such a good job with the children, to teach them. So that's why we moved away to a more Jewish city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Bedzin a lot? What do you remember about Bedzin?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it you remember? Remember being a big city? Do you remember having friends there?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of friends did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had friends. My age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do your bat mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't such a thing in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not for the-- not for the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers? Do you remember them having their bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- what about the holidays in your home?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when-- when-- when they had Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went to-- my parents went to a different city where there were more Jewish people and bought the kosher and matzo and all that, because over there where we lived, there was no such a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of people, other people, like cousins and uncles and aunts when they were-- Passover doing the Seders?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it just you and the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We had too-- My mother had a sister there, in the same city. I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother had a brother there? Is that what you said?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So your mother had a sister?

speaker: interviewer
text: And she would come and spend the Passovers with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And some maybe other people. I don't remember so well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the war started when-- when-- when the Germans occupied Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you remember about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember, I went to my cousin. I was going to stay with my cousin. I helped out sometimes in the house, sometimes in the-- she had a store, a grocery store. So I went there and we-- and I stayed with them. And when the war broke out, I had to go home because I didn't want to stay there because there was Oberschlesien, and there were more Germans then Jewish or other Polacks, so I went home to my parents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's how it start. Then they took my-- they took me away to a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before they took you to a camp, do you remember when the Germans occupied your city? I think-- were you in Bedzin at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a different-- it's a small city. We lived maybe an hour away from Bedzin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember occupying that city? When the Germans occupied that city?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about it?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the impression? Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you talking with your family about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's no good. We got to go someplace else. We want to run away from there, because I think, if I remember, that they came in there first then they came to Poland. This was before they came. We already had occupied. They already-- the people, most from Germany, occupied there, lived there. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: The war started on September 1, 1939, and they occupied right away your-- your-- your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. But before they occupied, we lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the people had to run away because the people from Silesia, from that town, told us, oh, they start to-- Polish, [POLISH] you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Names, yes. And throwing stones and all that thing. And my father said, well, it's no good. We got to move. So we--

speaker: interviewee
text: --moved. Yes. We moved out from there to Bedzin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bigger city and more Polish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: More Polish people and Jewish people. Because in there, they were leaving those Silesian people in Laurahutte. And they were speaking German mostly. So it's entirely different world in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start to understand German? You were taught German when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: --very young, but we spoke Polish and German. I did, because they were living there, Germans, and Polacks. I remember I went to mine cousin. They used to have a store, a grocery store, and I helped out. And they had a maid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the maid said-- I helped out in the store-- and the maid said, the war is start. When the war start already in Silesia, they came over from someplace. And I said-- she said, you wait. Wait till Hitler comes here. You know, he's going to give it to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening in Germany while you were in Poland before the war started? How Hitler was treating the Jews in Germany? Did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. That's why she said. She said, that maid, that Hitler's going to come to Poland, you know? And she said, you're not going to be so well when Hitler comes. So when they started like that, we just picked ourselves up and went to a different town where there were more Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any laws that the Germans started to give to get to Jews? Were there curfews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. For the Jewish people? Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did that start? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right after they occupied they started with the laws?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to be, 6 o'clock, we had to be in home. We couldn't go out anymore after 6:00.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until what time? From 6:00 to what?

speaker: interviewer
text: You could not be in the street?

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- did they make you wear a band with a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a little bit later, they made us wear the Juden band.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Juden band. A band with a Magen David.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Star of David on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I was taken, one time, too, I was taken to the jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- we didn't have-- in the winter time, we didn't have no water in the house. The water freezes, so I went for water to the corner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We had two buckets, and my-- that-- Band? The arm band?

speaker: interviewee
text: That slipped down. Slipped down to here, and a policeman sees me, and he said, where's your band? I didn't notice. And I said-- he said, did you know that you have to wear it here--

speaker: interviewee
text: German, yes. I said, well, I'm dragging the two buckets, so I couldn't have the band here. So it slipped down. Wrote my name, and after two weeks, there came a notice. I have to come Sosnowiec. That's a little bit farther than we used to live. And I didn't know what for. Oh, yes, I did. I'm sorry. Because of the band. It said I didn't wear the band. I said, I did wear the band, but the band slipped down. So there was no excuse. I had to go to that bigger town, Sosnowiec, and they put me right to jail. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to a police station there?

speaker: interviewer
text: And they put you in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put me right to jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go all by yourself or, did somebody accompany you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember if I went myself or with my mother. I just don't remember anymore. And they put me-- maybe it was my mom. And they put me to jail in there, but it was no jail. There was a big, very big, like a warehouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: A market there. And there were other prisoners that came in there. So many people, prisoners, sleeping on the floor. And the lights, headlights, were shining on them going around like that. And the policemen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Spotlights. Right. And they put me there, I didn't know for how long or what. And when the policemen took me, I had to go back. I didn't take a coat. I didn't have my coat. So my sister was home, and she threw the coat on me that I should have something, because they didn't let you have anything. So when they took me to that warehouse--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the market there, right. And my mother used to come sometimes-- every day, almost, and brought me some food package.

speaker: interviewer
text: Otherwise they would not give you--

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't go in. Yeah, they give us something, I think. And she couldn't go in in that warehouse, so she sees someone she knew that he is there too. So she would give him the package, the food. And the next time my mother came, she said, did you get the package? I said, what package? He took care of it. He ate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sitting next to him, I looked, and the package he opened up with cakes and things, it looks like my mom makes. So I said to him, oh, this is looking just like my mommy baking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, nothing. He ate it, and that's all. You know, he could--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he never gave you a piece?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. I just found out when my mom had come again, and she asked me if I got that package. I said, what package? She said, oh, no. I know. That's why I recognized your baking. That will make we cry now. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went-- you see, we used to live in a different town, and I used to be in a different town in that market there, so she had to come every day just to catch me. Every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean she would drive? She would drive there to see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when she came-- well, every day when she came, she would tell me goodbye, you know. And one day she came, she didn't see me no more, but they told her that I just left on the buses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It went away to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what concentration camp it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gross Sarne, I think. I think it was Gross Sarne.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just was in two, Gross Sarne or Schatzlar. Now I don't know which came first.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gross Sarne was the first camp you were in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took you on buses to go to the place?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a long-- long travel?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel a long time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was in a bus with you? A lot of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people. The ones they took from here, and one there. You know, from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Men and women. Because they sent us to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there in Gross Sarne?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was inside the camp, inside the camp, and they took us-- took me to peel the potatoes for the men who worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the kitchen? You worked in the kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. First I didn't work in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the potatoes outside. Then they liked me, and they took me to the kitchen, which was more work in the kitchen than to sit and peel the potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were doing the cooking there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just the preparing, because they had a German cook. So the preparing for the Jewish people I was doing. And the German there, that German woman and a German cook. So sometimes they told me to do something for them, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember physically what Gross Sarne looked like? What barracks or what kind of barracks were they where you slept?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a big barrack just with the girls. Just girls. And the other barracks the men occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, there were bunks in the barracks?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- do you remember how many people slept in a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 24 or more. I don't remember that quite well. But I slept down, and another friend, girl friend, slept up top bunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kind of food they gave you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not the best. But I remember I had more-- better food than the others because I worked in the kitchen. So sometimes I could even give to my friends some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what? What would you give them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bread, butter, whatever I had. Like, when you work in a kitchen, that's-- you can--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Gross Sarne?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed over a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then they sent us to a women's camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Woman's camps. Blechhammer, I think first.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a woman's camp at that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing in Blechhammer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, because we-- I wasn't there for long. Just a few days. That was like a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like a transit camp for you?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said a few days, and what did they do with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they sent us-- nothing. Just waiting to be sent away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they sent us to the woman's camp, Schatzlar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where Schatzlar is?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of camp was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbed wires all over the camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were making yarn for the soldiers for the coats. To make coats and uniforms, all kind of things for the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your specific job there at that factory to make yarn? What did you do there?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- what kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The machines were going, and the yarn was spinning. And I had to watch the yarn, when the yarn tore off. When the yarn tore off, I had to watch it and put it on again. And after 10 hours, I think we worked, we went to the barracks, we washed, we went to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say you 'washed,' they had washrooms?

speaker: interviewer
text: They had shower rooms also there? Did they? Did you take showers once in a while or never?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we did have shower rooms, too. Together with the other girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated? Were the SS women brutal or nice or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depends which one. Some were OK, some of them were not OK. So we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the ones who were not OK, and then tell me there ones who were OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones who were not OK, they were sometimes--

speaker: interviewee
text: With those whips. And some of them pretend they were going to hurt us. You know, they pretend.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they did not touch you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But most of them, the ones who didn't work so good, then they had to not so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they hit you? Where? In the face? In the body? Or where?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the body. On the body.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not too much, but I was hit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends in the camp that you could just--

speaker: interviewee
text: We make friends. We make friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Or they didn't live too far, in Poland, with us together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew them from before being arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We made friends in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were your age, so you had something in common?

speaker: interviewee
text: Old ones they didn't need. That I was young. All my young years, I give it for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop and change the tape, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't touch your hand to this--
